11.response: {"Q0":"I think that the key variable is risk appetite: I'm under the impression that the yellow planet and the green ice blocks served as neutral trials and probably the reward/no reward condition of the blue and red planets allowed you to observe if our risk behaviour (galaxy choice) changes when we expect a reward and we receive it and viceversa. I'm afraid it's way more complicated than that but as a psychology student I found this paradigm very fascinating."}

13.response: {"Q0":"I would have liked a progress bar, but I imagine that it was crucial that the participant wasn't aware of the duration (too long, too too long. Apart from that, it was very entertaining!"}

0.response: {"Q0":"I don't think I used any strategy. However, I noticed that I behaved differently if the planet would or wouldn't give me a reward. If I received a rock instead of a gem I would try to compensate the loss by risking more (if there was no 100% probability galaxy) or to be more conservative if there was an almost sure (90/100%) reward galaxy. I hope it makes sense."}

7.response: {"Q0":"I felt more relaxed because I wouldn't feel like I had to save gold in those trials where the planet that was supposed to return a gem would give me no reward. I always dug anyways, but since I got to know the overall result only when the block was over it felt simpler and less stressful"}

9.response: {"Q0":"I think yes: I have executed very precisely the planet task but I realised that the cost for digging wasn't that impactful. The galaxy task seemed more important to me."}

8.response: {"Q0":"I guess I chose what I thought was the best option. If the most probable reward was way smaller than the less probable one, and if the less probable one wasn't too improbable, then I would risk. Otherwise, I tried to choose the most secure option"}

12.response: {"Q0":"No issues; however - at least in my case - the experiment was very long and I was about to drop out towards the end."}
